entry.title: Re: Old Tile Still Appearing in SAP Build Work Zone After Complete Re‑Exposure from S/4HANA
entry.content:
@OmPrakashJha 

First, ensure that in the backend (tr-cd: /ui2/cdm3_exp_scope) that the roles are successfully exposed. You can check the exposed contents there.

Next, check the report in the content provider after refreshing. Does the updated role count match the number of roles actually updated? There are cases where refreshing looks successful but the count of updated content are zero.

If you are using content exposure version V2 in /ui2/cdm3_exp_scope, make sure to map the alias FLP_STANDALONE to the content provider. 

https://help.sap.com/docs/build-work-zone-standard-edition/sap-build-work-zone-standard-edition-on-china-shanghai-region/federation-of-remote-content-providers

entry.title: Missing plant from Starter System
entry.content:
We have just started our SAP Cloud ERP project and have started "Define Scope" step in the CBC. We have chosen and activated the scope item 1P7 - Cross plant transfer posting. In the Test Script, the transfer posting requires Plant DIGITS and plant DIGITS. 

However, when we go to the Organisational Structure step only plant DIGITS is available. Plant DIGITS is not there. Please advise how we can get Plant DIGITS in the Organisational Structure for the CBC for Starter System


------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Learn more about the SAP Support user and program here.

entry.title: Re: Missing plant from Starter System
entry.content:
Hi!

For scope item 1P7 – Cross-Plant Transfer Posting, the test script demonstrates the process using two plants (for example Plant 6910 and Plant 6920 when New Zealand is selected in your Evaluation Workspace) in order to illustrate a cross-plant scenario.

However, in the Organizational Structure step of SAP Central Business Configuration, it is expected that only the plant initially created in the workspace may be available (for example Plant 6910). If an additional plant is required to execute the scenario, it must be created manually in the organizational structure.

The Test Script documentation clarifies this point:

Flex.Org App or Set Up Organizational (SAP Central Business Configuration) extension

Note: The second set of organization entity IDs here is an example. In an evaluation project of the SAP Central Business Configuration environment, the ID follows the naming rule described in Using the Set Up Organizational Structure App.

This means that the second plant shown in the test script is only an example used to illustrate the cross-plant process, and it is not automatically created in the system.

If you are working in an Evaluation workspace connected to a Starter System, you can create an additional plant using the Set Up Organizational Structure app in SAP Central Business Configuration so that the scenario can be executed in SAP S/4HANA Cloud.

When creating organizational units in Evaluation workspaces, please follow these naming guidelines:

  • Distribution channels and divisions: create alphanumeric IDs with at least one alphabetic character.

  • Other organizational units (such as plants): create alphanumeric IDs where the last two characters are alphabetic.

Please refer to KBA: 3727800 - Cross-Plant Transfer Posting requires second Plant not available in SAP Central Business Configuration - SAP for Me

Therefore, if your scenario requires two plants, simply create an additional plant in the organizational structure following the naming guidelines for evaluation workspaces.

For additional details about the naming requirement or Organizational Structure, you may also refer to

  • 3487303 - Unable to create organizational unit due to naming requirement in SAP Central Business Configuration - SAP for Me
  • Using the Set Up Organizational Structure App | SAP Help Portal
  • Setting Up Organizational Structure | SAP Help Portal

Best Regards!

entry.title: Is SAP ABAP developer still a good career option in 2026 for someone starting fresh in IT?
entry.content:
Hi everyone,
I’m a 22-year-old Computer Science graduate currently navigating the transition from academia to the professional world. Over the past few months of job hunting, I’ve done some deep soul-searching regarding my technical strengths.
While I have a solid grasp of theory, system design, and "under-the-hood" fundamentals, I’ve realized that high-pressure, DSA-heavy competitive coding rounds aren't where I shine. I am a logical thinker who enjoys building structured, business-oriented solutions rather than solving abstract algorithmic puzzles.
This has led me to research the SAP Ecosystem, specifically ABAP, as a career path. I’m particularly drawn to the structured nature of enterprise software. However, I have a few questions for the experts here:
  1. Market Viability in 2026: For someone starting fresh today, is the demand for ABAP developers still strong, specifically with the industry-wide shift to S/4HANA?
  2. The AI Impact: How is SAP Joule and Generative AI changing the daily workflow of an ABAPer? Does a "Clean Core" strategy mean more or less work for custom developers?
  3. The "Full-Stack" SAP Dev: I see a lot of talk about SAP BTP, Fiori, and RAP (RESTful ABAP Programming). Should a fresher aim to be a "Pure ABAPer," or is it essential to start with a BTP-first mindset?
  4. Career Longevity: Coming from a non-competitive coding background, can I build a high-growth career here? Does the SAP world value system-design thinking over raw coding speed?
I’m eager to contribute to the enterprise space and want to ensure I’m investing my time in a future-proof stack,  I’d love to hear from those of you currently in the trenches.
Thank you for your time and guidance!

entry.title: Re: Export Software Collection/Export Customizing Transport
entry.content:
Hello @Ruby01 ,

Correct Transport Process in Public Cloud

DEV System
→ Create / Modify Custom Field
→ Publish Field
→ Add to Software Collection
→ Export Collection

TEST System
→ Import Software Collection

Quick Checklist

✔ Field is Published
✔ Field is assigned to a business context
✔ Field added to Software Collection manually
✔ Collection exported from DEV

 

Situation / QuestionExplanation, Resolution Steps & SAP Notes
Custom field was created/modified and published, but no transport request appearedIn S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, classical transport requests are not used. Instead, extensibility objects are moved using Software Collections. Publishing a custom field does not always automatically add it to a collection.
Change not visible in Export Software CollectionThe custom field must be manually added to a Software Collection before it can be exported from DEV to TEST.
Why the system did not prompt for transportThis is expected behavior in Public Cloud. Only developer extensibility objects or configuration items automatically trigger transport prompts. Custom fields must be collected manually.
How to resolve the issueFollow these steps: 1️⃣ Open Export Software Collection app 2️⃣ Create a new collection or edit existing one 3️⃣ Click Add Items 4️⃣ Select object type Custom Fields and Logic 5️⃣ Select your custom field 6️⃣ Add it to the collection 7️⃣ Export the collection to the target system
If the field still does not appearEnsure the field is: ✔ Published successfully ✔ Enabled for at least one business context ✔ Saved after publishing
Another reason it may not appearSometimes the object is already part of an existing collection created earlier. Check existing collections before creating a new one.
Where the object is storedCustom fields created via the Custom Fields and Logic app are stored as extensibility objects, which must be transported via Software Collections rather than configuration transports.
SAP Help ReferenceSAP Help → Transporting Software Collections in S/4HANA Cloud SAP Help → Custom Fields and Logic – Extensibility Transport
Relevant SAP Notes (Verify Applicability)📌 SAP Note 2831001 – Transport Mechanism in S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition 📌 SAP Note 3068705 – Extensibility Transport Issues with Software Collections 📌 SAP Note 3081998 – Custom Fields and Logic Transport Behavior

If you find my response helpful, share your feedback. This motivates me to serve you better.
Appreciate your support.
Thanks,
Divyesh

entry.title: Re: The task list in AFC does not sort based on task id,...
entry.content:
Hello @AniruddhaMagar ,

The Task ID column in AFC is mainly an identifier, not the driver of execution or display order.
AFC prioritizes task dependencies and logical execution sequence, which is why the list may appear unsorted.

The Best practice is to Control order through task dependencies and task groups instead of relying on Task ID sorting.

 

Situation / QuestionExplanation, Workarounds & SAP Notes
AFC task list appears jumbled and not sorted by Task IDIn Advanced Financial Closing (AFC), the task list is not always sorted by Task ID by default. The UI often sorts based on internal sequence / execution dependency, not the numeric Task ID visible on screen.
Sorting manually by column does not workIn some AFC versions, the Task ID column is treated as a text field, so sorting may be alphabetical instead of numeric (e.g., 5-1, 5-10, 5-2). Additionally, task dependencies may override sorting behavior.
Why SAP behaves this wayAFC prioritizes task execution logic and dependencies rather than visual ordering. If tasks have predecessor/successor relationships, the system preserves that logical sequence in the UI.
Most common cause of “random order”Task list created with parallel tasks or dependencies. The UI groups tasks according to dependency graph instead of simple Task ID order.
Recommended solutionUse task groups or sequence numbers in the task template to control logical order instead of relying on Task ID sorting.
WorkaroundExport the task list to Excel (or use reporting) if sequential numeric review of Task IDs is required.
Configuration checkVerify task dependencies in the Closing Template (Define Task Lists in AFC). If unnecessary dependencies exist, removing them may allow more predictable ordering.
UI limitationCurrent AFC Fiori UI does not always guarantee stable numeric sorting of Task IDs due to the internal execution order model.
SAP Help ReferenceSAP Help Portal → Advanced Financial Closing – Task Templates and Task Lists

If you find my response helpful, share your feedback. This motivates me to serve you better.
Appreciate your support.
Thanks,
Divyesh

entry.title: Re: Claude Code via MCP: Poor man's Joule, or a practical tool?
entry.content:
Day 7, an evening session

Doubling down: 60% there.

Tonight I doubled down on the idea of vibe-coding an entire Fiori web app, both back-end and front-end parts. To my continued surprise, the back-end was easier than the front-end. Never would have expected that. Colour me amazed.

I continued with my new best friend OpenAI Codex and asked him to finish all back-end artifacts of the RAP Application for the Northwind Traders - Extended version. It went without a glitch, nothing to mention there. He would finish a step, then say "I suggest we do this next", I would say "Yes please" and go about my other business while he worked.

Then I published the entire back-end part to this GitHub Repo using abapGit.

Next I asked Codex to build a local freestyle Fiori app. As all ABAP Objects were in the same Package, I just pointed him to that Package.

But not without a plan, no sir, never again without a plan. Having a plan makes all the difference. It forces the AI to think, but more importantly, it forces you to think.

Building the app took him about 26 minutes (Codex doesn't report the time, so this is approximate).

Did it work? Kind of. Maybe 35-40%-ish. There were all kinds of bugs like dead navigation links, Object Pages without data, values not being saved after updating. I started testing and asking for bug fixes one by one, until I got fed up. I noticed that he installed and used Playwright,  so I came up with the following promt:

Please audit the app end-to-end with Playwright. First inventory every navigable flow and reproduce the broken ones, then patch them and rerun the same audit until the detail pages and core links all resolve with live data.

Plan:

Systematic UI audit and repair for the local SAPUI5 app:

  • Inspect current routes, controllers, and major list/detail flows to define the audit scope.
  • Run Playwright-based audit across navigation and detail pages to capture broken links or empty bindings.
  • Fix the identified routing, binding, and interaction defects.
  • Rerun the audit and targeted live checks until all core flows pass.
  • Do a second full Playwright sweep focused on create/edit/delete dialogs and in-detail actions.

 

This took about half an hour and brought the app to maybe 60-65% usability. I have published it as-is in this GitHub Repo, here are a few screenshots of what it looks like:

    

I still think it should be possible to build an entire Fiori app with AI, but not by vibe-coding. A more serious approach will be needed, and I have started working on it.

 

A small step towards Agentic Engineering

My next setup will start incorporating some elements of Agentic Engineering (see yesterday's blog). I decided to start with Agent Hierarchy, then Skills.

Here I switched back to Claude, which, I believe, is more advanced in this field. After a short conversation he helped me create this Agent Hierarchy. I have yet put it to test. What is not clear to me is how shall I know that it worked - I'd like to have something measurable.

Next I want to concentrate on Skills. When I have them, I might delete the existing web-app and re-create it, or perhaps use another one of the free Database Schemas, such as Chinook, and build an app around it.

When it comes to prompting, I'm thinking the back-end will require at least 2 prompts: One for planning, one for execution. I'm hoping the Agent Hierarchy will help with the constant filling up of the context window, as well as with the cost (plus perhaps it might bring a small improvement on speed, as Haiku is faster than Sonnet).

The front-end will require at least 3 prompts: Plan, execute, audit & correct. In addition, I'd like to have a conversation with Claude Chat on what kind of automated testing (if any) might guarantee a higher rate of completeness than those 60-65% I have been able to achieve so far.

 

I am slowly easing into the mindset of doing things through AI. For example: 

It's fun. I have a feeling I have learned more in the last 7 days than in the 3, if not 5 years before that. For sure I won't be able to maintain this pace for long, but while it lasts... 😊

 

P.S. 7,2 million tokens tonight, OMG, that's like a million gazillion Euros! 😱

OK, not million gazillion: 38,50. But still!

(I didn't actually pay anything extra, my 20 EUR Pro plan covered it. OpenAI is being extremely generous with token limits and rate limits right now, as they are trying to take Anthropic's crown in the Agentic Development space. Just cancel your Netflix subscription, and redirect the funds thusly saved into an AI Pro subscription. Conversing with AI is way more fun than watching Netflix 😁).

entry.title: HCM Brasil: CNPJ Alfanumérico - Fase 1 Concluída!
entry.content:
Boa tarde pessoal,

É com satisfação que anunciamos a conclusão de todas as entregas relacionadas à Fase 1 do projeto CNPJ Alfanumérico no módulo HCM.

O que isso significa?
Nesta etapa, realizamos adaptações técnicas importantes para que o HCM dê os primeiros passos rumo ao suporte ao CNPJ com letras.
Atenção: A total adequação do módulo HCM ao CNPJ alfanumérico só será finalizada na Fase 2 do projeto, prevista para entrega até 31/03/2026:
➡️ “Brazil's Tax Reform - Alphanumeric CNPJ - HCM Impacts - Phase 2”

Além disso, lembre-se que outros módulos da solução SAP também exigirão ajustes antes que seja possível cadastrar e utilizar plenamente o novo formato de CNPJ no sistema.
Para não perder nenhuma atualização, acompanhe sempre a SAP Leading Note 3714871.

Entregas Desta Fase

Vale destacar que as mudanças para adaptar o HCM ao CNPJ alfanumérico envolveram um grande número de objetos do sistema — tabelas, domínios, relatórios e eventos —, o que amplia significativamente o impacto desta fase. Todas as alterações podem afetar tanto estruturas padrão quanto customizações existentes. Confira abaixo as SAP Notes já entregues e um breve resumo de suas alterações:

  • 3665009: Prerequisite objects for SAP Note 3665158
  • 3665158: eSocial - Preparation for alphanumeric CNPJs
  • 3709740: Prerequisite objects for SAP Note 3725117
  • 3725117: Alphanumeric CNPJ - Changes to Transparent Tables and Views
  • 3707858: Prerequisite objects for SAP Note 3705711
  • 3705711: Alphanumeric CNPJ - Implementation for HCMBR Reports
  • 3701092: Prerequisite objects for SAP Note 3700999
  • 3700999: eSocial - Alphanumeric CNPJ - Changes in standard events
  • 3709087: eSocial - Alphanumeric CNPJ - Changes in limited scope events
  • 3721621: eSocial - Alphanumeric CNPJ - Guidance to review the BAdIs impacted

Resumindo as principais mudanças:

  • Criação do novo elemento de dados: HRPADBR_CNPJ (CHAR14)
  • Alteração do campo nrInsc nos eventos do eSocial: de NUMC para CHAR
  • Ajustes em tabelas e views (T7BRC0, T7BRTAXWRKPLC, etc.)
  • Adequação de relatórios legais e programas para aceitar o novo formato
  • Orientações para revisar/atualizar customizações e evitar inconsistências

Checklist – Como você deve se preparar

  • Confira todos os objetos impactados: Para cada alteração, verifique os objetos relacionados na respectiva SAP Note. Utilize também a SAP Leading Note para acessar a lista completa e consolidada de todos os objetos afetados.
  • Identifique BAdIs e custom codes (“Z”) que processam valores de CNPJ/nrInsc.
  • Se necessário, implemente a chamada ao método CL_HRPAYBR_INSC_NUM_NORMALIZER=>STANDARDIZE(iv_insc_num).
  • Realize testes para garantir a padronização do novo formato.
  • Acompanhe a Fase 2 (previsão: 31/03/2026) e futuras entregas.
  • Compartilhe este conteúdo com sua equipe técnica.

Padronização de CNPJ – Dica Técnica

A SAP disponibilizou a classe CL_HRPAYBR_INSC_NUM_NORMALIZER que padroniza o valor de CNPJ/nrInsc, evitando rejeições ou inconsistências por formato.

Como funciona?

  • Adiciona zeros à esquerda se necessário
  • Mantém o valor se já estiver correto
  • Ignora se valor for “zero” total

Utilize sempre esse método em suas customizações!

Como acompanhar as novidades?

  • Todas as atualizações estão na SAP Note 3714871 - Tax Reform: Alphanumeric CNPJ Leading SAP Note.
  • Acompanhe o item “Impactos do CNPJ Alfanumérico na Reforma Tributária”
  • Consulte também:
    • Tax Reform - Alphanumeric CNPJ - Normative Instruction No. 2229, of October 15, 2024
    • Tax Reform - Joint Technical Note 2025.001 - Alphanumeric CNPJ

Importante: Acompanhe a entrega da Fase 2 (prevista para 31/03/2026) para garantir total compatibilidade no cadastro e processamento do novo CNPJ no HCM. Para garantir um cadastro consistente de CNPJ alfanumérico em todo o sistema, é fundamental acompanhar a Leading Note. Alterações em outros módulos também serão necessárias.

Gostou deste conteúdo?

Deixe seu like, compartilhe com colegas e registre dúvidas, experiências ou sugestões nos comentários!
Siga a tag HCM Payroll Brazil na SAP Community para se manter sempre atualizado.

Um grande abraço e seguimos juntos!

Anderson Marques

entry.title: Build Your Own SAP MCP Server in Minutes with odata-mcp-proxy
entry.content:
Introduction

In my previous blog post, I introduced the OData MCP Proxy, a configuration-driven framework that turns any OData V2 or REST API into an MCP server for AI assistants: https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-members/odata-mcp-proxy-introduction/ba-p/14348684

In this post, I'll show you exactly how to use the odata-mcp-proxy npm package to build your own MCP server from scratch.

The key insight: you don't write any code. You create a JSON configuration file that describes your APIs and the proxy generates all the MCP tools automatically. Let me walk you through it with real examples from the MCP servers I've built.

Prerequisites

  • Node.js 18+ (20+ recommended)
  • An SAP BTP account (for cloud deployment)
  • Cloud Foundry CLI and MBT Build Tool (for BTP deployment)
  • API documentation for the SAP service you want to expose

Step 1: Initialize Your Project

Create a new Node.js project and install the proxy:

mkdir my-sap-mcp-server
cd my-sap-mcp-server
npm init -y
npm install odata-mcp-proxy

Update your package.json scripts:

{
  "name": "my-sap-mcp-server",
  "version": "1.0.0",
  "scripts": {
    "start": "odata-mcp-proxy --config my-api-config.json",
    "build:btp": "mbt build",
    "deploy:btp": "cf deploy mta_archives/my-sap-mcp-server_1.0.0.mtar"
  },
  "dependencies": {
    "odata-mcp-proxy": "^1.0.0"
  }
}

That's your entire package.json. One dependency, three scripts. The odata-mcp-proxy package provides a CLI binary that reads your config and runs the server.

Step 2: Create the API Configuration

This is where the magic happens. You describe your APIs in a JSON file and the proxy generates all the MCP tools from it. Let me walk through the structure using real examples.

Server Metadata

{
  "server": {
    "name": "my-sap-mcp-server",
    "version": "1.0.0",
    "description": "MCP server for SAP Service XYZ"
  },
  "apis": [ ... ]
}

API Definitions

Each entry in the apis array represents one backend API. You can define multiple APIs, each with its own destination, path prefix and entity sets.

Example from the CI MCP Server (SAP Cloud Integration OData V2 API):

{
  "name": "cpi",
  "destination": "CPI_DESTINATION",
  "pathPrefix": "/api/v1",
  "csrfProtected": true,
  "entitySets": [ ... ]
}

Example from the BTP MCP Server (REST APIs — note csrfProtected is false):

{
  "name": "cis-accounts",
  "destination": "BTP_ACCOUNTS_SERVICE",
  "pathPrefix": "/accounts/v1",
  "csrfProtected": false,
  "entitySets": [ ... ]
}

Entity Set Definitions

Each entity set becomes a group of MCP tools. Here's a full example:

{
  "entitySet": "IntegrationPackages",
  "description": "Integration packages containing iFlows and artifacts",
  "category": "integration-content",
  "keys": [{ "name": "Id", "type": "string" }],
  "operations": {
    "list": true,
    "get": true,
    "create": true,
    "update": true,
    "delete": true
  },
  "filterableProperties": ["Id", "Name", "Vendor", "Mode", "Version"],
  "navigationProperties": [
    {
      "name": "IntegrationDesigntimeArtifacts",
      "description": "Design-time artifacts in this package",
      "isCollection": true
    }
  ]
}

This single definition generates 6 MCP tools:

Generated Tool

HTTP Method

What It Does

IntegrationPackages_list

GET

List all packages with OData query support

IntegrationPackages_get

GET

Get a single package by key

IntegrationPackages_create

POST

Create a new package

IntegrationPackages_update

PATCH

Update a package by key

IntegrationPackages_delete

DELETE

Delete a package by key

IntegrationPackages_IntegrationDesigntimeArtifacts_list

GET

List artifacts in a package

Understanding Key Fields

csrfProtected: Set to true for SAP OData V2 APIs (they use CSRF tokens). Set to false for REST APIs (BTP Core Services, Cloud Foundry V3, AI Core). The proxy handles CSRF token fetching automatically when enabled.

urlPath: Use this when the actual URL path differs from the entitySet name. For example, the Cloud Foundry API uses lowercase paths (service_instances) while you might want PascalCase tool names (ServiceInstances). Example:

{
  "entitySet": "ServiceInstances",
  "urlPath": "service_instances",
  ...
}

Parameterized paths: For nested REST resources, use curly braces. Example from AI Core:

{
  "entitySet": "Executables",
  "urlPath": "scenarios/{scenarioId}/executables",
  ...
}

Step 3: Configure Authentication

For Local Development

Set environment variables based on your destination name. The prefix is the destination name in uppercase with non-alphanumeric characters replaced by underscores:

# For destination "CPI_DESTINATION"
CPI_DESTINATION_BASE_URL=https://tenant.it-cpi018.cfapps.eu10.hana.ondemand.com
CPI_DESTINATION_TOKEN_URL=https://subdomain.authentication.eu10.hana.ondemand.com/oauth/token
CPI_DESTINATION_CLIENT_ID=your-client-id
CPI_DESTINATION_CLIENT_SECRET=your-client-secret

Alternatively, create a default-env.json file with VCAP_SERVICES bindings (as you would for any BTP-bound application running locally) by providing a default-env.json file and create a destination in BTP:

{
  "VCAP_SERVICES": {
    "xsuaa": [
      {
        "name": "mcp-uaa",
        "label": "xsuaa",
        "tags": ["xsuaa"],
        "credentials":<btp xssuaa credentials>
      }
    ],
    "destination": [
      {
        "name": "mcp-dest",
        "label": "destination",
        "tags": ["destination"],
        "credentials":<btp destination credentials>
      }
    ],
    "connectivity": [
      {
        "name": "mcp-conn",
        "label": "connectivity",
        "tags": ["connectivity"],
        "credentials": <btp connectivity credentials>
      }
    ]
  }
}

 

For BTP Deployment

Create BTP Destinations in the SAP BTP Cockpit pointing to your target APIs with OAuth2 client credentials authentication. The proxy uses the SAP Cloud SDK to resolve these destinations automatically when VCAP_SERVICES is present.

Step 4: Run Locally

npm start

The server starts on port 4004 by default. You can test it with any MCP client, or use the MCP Inspector for debugging:

npx @anthropic-ai/mcp-inspector

Step 5: Deploy to SAP BTP

Create three files for BTP deployment:

mta.yaml

_schema-version: "3.1"
ID: my-sap-mcp-server
version: 1.0.0

modules:
  - name: my-sap-mcp-server
    type: nodejs
    path: .
    parameters:
      memory: 512M
      disk-quota: 1G
      buildpack: nodejs_buildpack
      health-check-type: http
      health-check-http-endpoint: /health
    build-parameters:
      builder: custom
      commands:
        - npm install --production
    requires:
      - name: my-mcp-server-dest
      - name: my-mcp-server-conn
      - name: my-mcp-server-uaa

resources:
  - name: my-mcp-server-dest
    type: org.cloudfoundry.managed-service
    parameters:
      service: destination
      service-plan: lite
  - name: my-mcp-server-conn
    type: org.cloudfoundry.managed-service
    parameters:
      service: connectivity
      service-plan: lite
  - name: my-mcp-server-uaa
    type: org.cloudfoundry.managed-service
    parameters:
      service: xsuaa
      service-plan: application
      path: xs-security.json

xs-security.json

{
  "xsappname": "my-sap-mcp-server",
  "tenant-mode": "dedicated",
  "scopes": [
    { "name": "$XSAPPNAME.read", "description": "Read access" },
    { "name": "$XSAPPNAME.write", "description": "Write access" },
    { "name": "$XSAPPNAME.admin", "description": "Admin access" }
  ],
  "role-templates": [
    {
      "name": "MCPViewer",
      "scope-references": ["$XSAPPNAME.read"]
    },
    {
      "name": "MCPEditor",
      "scope-references": ["$XSAPPNAME.read", "$XSAPPNAME.write"]
    },
    {
      "name": "MCPAdmin",
      "scope-references": ["$XSAPPNAME.read", "$XSAPPNAME.write", "$XSAPPNAME.admin"]
    }
  ]
}

Build and Deploy

# Build the MTA archive
npm run build:btp

# Deploy to Cloud Foundry
npm run deploy:btp

Real-World Examples

I've built five MCP servers using this exact pattern, each for a different SAP service:

MCP Server

SAP Service

Entity Sets

GitHub

Blog post

ci-mcp-server

SAP Cloud Integration

32

https://github.com/lemaiwo/ci-mcp-server 

https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-members/ci-mcp-server-managing-sap-cloud-integration-with-ai-assistants/ba-p/14348688

ai-core-mcp-server

SAP AI Core

19

https://github.com/lemaiwo/ai-core-mcp-server 

https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-members/ai-core-mcp-server-managing-sap-ai-core-with-ai-assistants/ba-p/14348695

btp-mcp-server

BTP Core Services

11

https://github.com/lemaiwo/btp-mcp-server 

https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-members/btp-mcp-server-managing-your-sap-btp-landscape-with-ai-assistants/ba-p/14348692

btp-auditlog-mcp-server

BTP Audit Log

1

https://github.com/lemaiwo/btp-auditlog-mcp-server 

https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-members/btp-audit-log-mcp-server-security-monitoring-with-ai-assistants/ba-p/14348696#M178015

btp-cf-mcp-server

Cloud Foundry V3

28

https://github.com/lemaiwo/btp-cf-mcp-server 

https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-members/btp-cloud-foundry-mcp-server-managing-cf-environments-with-ai/ba-p/14348694

Each of these is a zero-code project: just a JSON config, an MTA descriptor and an XSUAA security definition. The odata-mcp-proxy package does all the heavy lifting.

Tips for Building Your Own

  • Start from the API documentation: Identify the entity sets, their keys and available operations. Map each endpoint to an entity set definition in your config.
  • Set csrfProtected correctly: OData V2 APIs (like SAP CPI) use CSRF tokens, set it to true. REST APIs (like BTP Core Services, Cloud Foundry, AI Core) don't — set it to false.
  • Use urlPath when path casing differs: REST APIs often use lowercase/snake_case paths, but you want PascalCase tool names for readability.
  • Test locally first: Use environment variables or default-env.json before deploying to BTP.
  • Use categories for organization: Group related entity sets under categories. Users can filter enabled categories via the ENABLED_API_CATEGORIES environment variable.

Conclusion

The odata-mcp-proxy npm package makes it trivial to create MCP servers for any SAP API. No TypeScript expertise needed, no custom HTTP handling, no OAuth2 token management code, just describe your APIs in JSON and let the proxy do the rest.

In the following blog posts, I'll dive into each of the five MCP servers I've built, showing the specific configurations, destination setup and deployment details for each SAP service.

Links

  • OData MCP Proxy (GitHub): https://github.com/lemaiwo/odata-mcp-proxy
  • OData MCP Proxy (npm): https://www.npmjs.com/package/odata-mcp-proxy

entry.title: OData MCP Proxy - Introduction
entry.content:
Introduction

What if your AI assistant could directly talk to your SAP systems? Not through copy-pasting API responses, but through a standardized protocol that lets AI understand your API landscape and make calls on your behalf?

That's exactly what the OData MCP Proxy enables. It's an open-source, configuration-driven server that exposes any OData V2 or REST API as MCP (Model Context Protocol) tools, making them instantly accessible to AI assistants like Claude, Cursor, or any MCP-compatible client.

In this blog post, I'll walk you through what the OData MCP Proxy is, how it works under the hood and why it matters for the SAP ecosystem.

What is the Model Context Protocol (MCP)?

The Model Context Protocol is an open standard (originally developed by Anthropic) that provides a standardized way for AI assistants to interact with external tools and data sources. The easiest way to understand MCP is through a familiar analogy: HTTP is the standard protocol that connects users (via web browsers) to web servers. MCP plays the same role for AI, it's the standard protocol that connects AI agents to external capabilities. Where a web browser is a "dumb" renderer that displays whatever the server sends, an AI agent is a "smart" browser that understands context, decides which tools to call and acts on your behalf.

MCP defines a simple contract: a server registers "tools" (functions with typed inputs and outputs) and an AI client discovers and invokes those tools during conversations. The AI decides when and how to call which tool based on the user's natural language request. Just as a web server hosts pages, APIs and media, an MCP server hosts tools and resources allowing the AI agent to navigate through them intelligently.

 

What is the OData MCP Proxy?

The OData MCP Proxy is an open-source npm package that turns any OData V2 or REST API into an MCP server. You describe your APIs in a JSON configuration file and the proxy automatically generates MCP tools for every entity set and operation you define.

No custom code required. Just configuration.

A complete package.json , just one dependency and a start script:

The best analogy in the SAP world: it's like the SAP Application Router (approuter). The approuter is a ready-made runtime you configure with a routing descriptor, you don't write the HTTP proxy logic yourself. Similarly, the OData MCP Proxy is a ready-made MCP runtime you configure with an API descriptor.

Key Features

  • Configuration-driven tool registration, no code needed
  • Full CRUD support (list, get, create, update, delete) per entity set
  • OData V2 query capabilities ($filter, $select, $expand, $orderby, $top, $skip)
  • Navigation property traversal for related entities
  • Dual transport: HTTP (for cloud deployment) and stdio (for local Claude Desktop) - although only HTTP is valuable when deployed on BTP
  • Automatic OAuth2 token management via SAP BTP Destination Service
  • XSUAA integration with role-based access control (not entirely implemented yet)
  • Category-based filtering to enable/disable API groups
  • Deployable to SAP BTP Cloud Foundry

Architecture: How It Works

One of the key strengths of the OData MCP Proxy is that it is designed to run natively on SAP BTP Cloud Foundry. This means it plugs directly into BTP's security and connectivity infrastructure, Destination Service for credential management, XSUAA for role-based access control and Connectivity Service for on-premise access via Cloud Connector.

Here's the full architecture:

The proxy runs as a Cloud Foundry application on SAP BTP, where it leverages the platform's built-in services for security, credential management and connectivity. This is a key advantage: instead of managing OAuth tokens and credentials yourself, BTP handles it all.

When the proxy starts, it reads the JSON configuration file and registers MCP tools for every entity set and operation defined in it. Each tool follows a generic thin-proxy pattern: tools expose path, body and headers parameters and the AI constructs the OData path and query string.

For example, when a user asks "Show me all integration packages that start with SAP", the AI assistant:

  1. Identifies that IntegrationPackages_list is the right tool
  2. Constructs the OData filter: $filter=startswith(Name,'SAP')
  3. Calls the tool with path: "?$filter=startswith(Name,'SAP')"
  4. The proxy routes this to the SAP CPI OData API and returns the result

The Configuration File

The heart of the proxy is the api-config.json file. Here's the structure:

{
  "server": {
    "name": "my-mcp-server",
    "version": "1.0.0",
    "description": "My SAP MCP Server"
  },
  "apis": [
    {
      "name": "my-api",
      "destination": "MY_DESTINATION",
      "pathPrefix": "/api/v1",
      "csrfProtected": true,
      "entitySets": [
        {
          "entitySet": "Products",
          "urlPath": "Products",
          "description": "Product master data",
          "category": "master-data",
          "keys": [{ "name": "Id", "type": "string" }],
          "operations": {
            "list": true, "get": true,
            "create": false, "update": false, "delete": false
          }
        }
      ]
    }
  ]
}

Key configuration fields:

Field

Purpose

server

MCP server metadata (name, version, description)

apis[].destination

BTP Destination name (or env var prefix for local dev)

apis[].pathPrefix

Base URL path prepended to all requests

apis[].csrfProtected

true for OData V2 (CSRF tokens), false for REST APIs

entitySets[].entitySet

Tool name prefix (PascalCase) — becomes e.g. Products_list

entitySets[].urlPath

Actual URL path segment (can differ from entitySet name)

entitySets[].keys

Entity key properties for GET/UPDATE/DELETE operations

entitySets[].operations

Boolean flags controlling which CRUD tools are generated

entitySets[].filterableProperties

Properties that support OData $filter

entitySets[].navigationProperties

Related entities (generates additional _list tools)

Transport Modes

The proxy supports two transport modes:

HTTP Transport (default)

An Express-based HTTP server running on port 4004 by default. Uses MCP's Streamable HTTP protocol with Server-Sent Events (SSE) for real-time notifications. This is what you use for cloud deployment on SAP BTP Cloud Foundry.

stdio Transport (only locally)

Reads and writes over standard I/O. This is for local integration with Claude Desktop, Claude starts the proxy as a subprocess and communicates directly through stdin/stdout.

Authentication & Security

The proxy integrates with SAP BTP's security infrastructure:

  • BTP Destination Service: Credentials are resolved from BTP Destinations. The SAP Cloud SDK handles OAuth2 token fetch, caching and refresh automatically.
  • XSUAA: Optional JWT-based authentication with three role levels — MCPViewer (read), MCPEditor (read+write), MCPAdmin (full access). This is not fully implemented yet
  • CSRF Protection: Automatically managed for OData V2 APIs. Disabled for REST APIs via the csrfProtected flag.
  • Local development: When VCAP_SERVICES is absent, credentials are read from environment variables using a naming convention derived from the destination name.

Built for SAP BTP

The OData MCP Proxy is not just compatible with SAP BTP, it is built for it. Running on BTP Cloud Foundry gives you enterprise-grade infrastructure out of the box:

  • Destination Service: Centralized credential management. Define your API destinations once in BTP and the proxy resolves them automatically, including OAuth2 token lifecycle (fetch, cache, refresh).
  • XSUAA: Role-based access control via JWT tokens. Control which users can read, write, or administer through your MCP server without writing any auth code.
  • Connectivity Service: Reach on-premise SAP systems through the Cloud Connector, no VPN or firewall changes required.
  • MTA Deployment: Standard Multi-Target Application descriptor. Build with mbt build, deploy with cf deploy. Service bindings, routing and scaling are all declarative.

This means you can expose your SAP APIs to AI assistants in a secure, governed and scalable way, exactly how you'd expect enterprise software to work on BTP.

Open Source & npm Package

The OData MCP Proxy is available as:

  • GitHub: https://github.com/lemaiwo/odata-mcp-proxy
  • npm: https://www.npmjs.com/package/odata-mcp-proxy

Install it with npm install odata-mcp-proxy and start building your own MCP servers today.

What's Next?

In the next blog post, I'll show you step-by-step how to create your own MCP server using the odata-mcp-proxy npm package. We'll go from zero to a working MCP server in under 10 minutes: https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-members/build-your-own-sap-mcp-server-in-minutes-with-odata-mcp-proxy/ba-p/14348685

I've already built five MCP servers using this proxy for different SAP services: SAP Cloud Integration, SAP AI Core, BTP Core Services, BTP Audit Log and Cloud Foundry. Each of these will get its own dedicated blog post with configuration details and deployment guides.

I've built five MCP servers using this exact pattern, each for a different SAP service:

MCP Server

SAP Service

Entity Sets

GitHub

Blog post

ci-mcp-server

SAP Cloud Integration

32

https://github.com/lemaiwo/ci-mcp-server 

https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-members/ci-mcp-server-managing-sap-cloud-integration-with-ai-assistants/ba-p/14348688

ai-core-mcp-server

SAP AI Core

19

https://github.com/lemaiwo/ai-core-mcp-server 

https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-members/ai-core-mcp-server-managing-sap-ai-core-with-ai-assistants/ba-p/14348695

btp-mcp-server

BTP Core Services

11

https://github.com/lemaiwo/btp-mcp-server 

https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-members/btp-mcp-server-managing-your-sap-btp-landscape-with-ai-assistants/ba-p/14348692

btp-auditlog-mcp-server

BTP Audit Log

1

https://github.com/lemaiwo/btp-auditlog-mcp-server 

https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-members/btp-audit-log-mcp-server-security-monitoring-with-ai-assistants/ba-p/14348696#M178015

btp-cf-mcp-server

Cloud Foundry V3

28

https://github.com/lemaiwo/btp-cf-mcp-server 

https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-members/btp-cloud-foundry-mcp-server-managing-cf-environments-with-ai/ba-p/14348694

Links

  • OData MCP Proxy (GitHub): https://github.com/lemaiwo/odata-mcp-proxy
  • OData MCP Proxy (npm): https://www.npmjs.com/package/odata-mcp-proxy
  • Model Context Protocol: https://modelcontextprotocol.io

entry.title: CI MCP Server: Managing SAP Cloud Integration with AI Assistants
entry.content:
Introduction

SAP Cloud Integration (part of SAP Integration Suite) has a comprehensive OData V2 API that covers everything from integration content management to message monitoring, security material, and partner directory operations. But working with these APIs typically means writing scripts, using Postman, or navigating the Web UI manually.

What if you could simply ask your AI assistant: "Show me all failed messages from the last hour" or "Deploy the latest version of my iFlow"? That's what the CI MCP Server enables.

Built on the odata-mcp-proxy npm package, the CI MCP Server exposes all 32 entity sets of the SAP CPI OData V2 API as MCP tools,  with zero custom code. Read more about the odata-mcp-proxy here: https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-members/odata-mcp-proxy-introduction/ba-p/14348684

What Can You Do With It?

The CI MCP Server gives AI assistants complete programmatic access to your SAP Cloud Integration tenant:

Integration Content Lifecycle

  • Create, read, update, and delete integration packages
  • Manage iFlows, value mappings, message mappings, and script collections
  • Track build and deploy status
  • View runtime artifact versions

Message Monitoring

  • Query message processing logs with rich filtering (status, date range, sender, receiver)
  • Drill into error details, attachments, and adapter attributes
  • Inspect custom header properties
  • Check exactly-once processing (ID mapping and idempotent repository)

Data Stores & Variables

  • Browse and delete data store entries
  • Inspect runtime variables
  • Monitor JMS queues (broker capacity, queue depth)

Security Material

  • Manage keystore entries, certificates, and SSH keys
  • CRUD operations on user credentials and OAuth2 client credentials
  • Manage secure parameters and certificate-to-user mappings
  • Configure access policies with artifact references

Partner Directory

  • Full CRUD on partners, string/binary parameters
  • Manage alternative partners and authorized users

API Categories & Entity Sets

The 32 entity sets are organized into 6 categories:

Category

Entity Sets

Description

integration-content

8

Packages, artifacts, build status, custom tags

message-processing-logs

3

Message logs with 5 navigation properties

message-stores

6

Data stores, variables, JMS queues, number ranges

log-files

2

Runtime logs and log archives

security-content

8

Keystores, credentials, certificates, access policies

partner-directory

5

Partners, parameters, authorized users

You can selectively enable categories using the ENABLED_API_CATEGORIES environment variable. For example, to only expose monitoring tools:

ENABLED_API_CATEGORIES=message-processing-logs,log-files

Configuration

The entire server is defined in ci-api-config.json:

{
  "server": {
    "name": "ci-mcp-server",
    "version": "1.0.0",
    "description": "SAP Cloud Integration MCP Server"
  },
  "apis": [{
    "name": "cpi",
    "destination": "CPI_DESTINATION",
    "pathPrefix": "/api/v1",
    "csrfProtected": true,
    "entitySets": [
      {
        "entitySet": "IntegrationPackages",
        "description": "Integration packages ...",
        "category": "integration-content",
        "keys": [{ "name": "Id", "type": "string" }],
        "operations": { "list": true, "get": true, "create": true, "update": true, "delete": true },
        "filterableProperties": ["Id", "Name", "Vendor", "Mode", "Version"]
      },
      ... (32 entity sets total)
    ]
  }]
}

Key configuration choices:

  • csrfProtected: true — SAP CPI uses OData V2 with CSRF token protection. The proxy automatically fetches and manages CSRF tokens for all mutating operations (POST, PATCH, DELETE).
  • Single destination — All 32 entity sets share one CPI tenant destination with the /api/v1 path prefix.
  • Navigation properties — MessageProcessingLogs has 5 navigation properties (Attachments, ErrorInformations, AdapterAttributes, CustomHeaderProperties, MessageStoreEntries), each generating an additional _list tool for drilling into related data.

Setting Up the BTP Destination

Create a destination in your SAP BTP Cockpit:

Property

Value

Name

CPI_DESTINATION

Type

HTTP

URL

https://<tenant>.it-cpi018.cfapps.<region>.hana.ondemand.com

Authentication

OAuth2ClientCredentials

Client ID

From your CPI service key

Client Secret

From your CPI service key

Token Service URL

https://<subdomain>.authentication.<region>.hana.ondemand.com/oauth/token

To get the service key credentials, create a service instance of "Process Integration Runtime" with plan "api" in your BTP subaccount, then create a service key.

Local Development

For local testing without BTP, set environment variables:

CPI_DESTINATION_BASE_URL=https://tenant.it-cpi018.cfapps.eu10.hana.ondemand.com
CPI_DESTINATION_TOKEN_URL=https://subdomain.authentication.eu10.hana.ondemand.com/oauth/token
CPI_DESTINATION_CLIENT_ID=your-client-id
CPI_DESTINATION_CLIENT_SECRET=your-client-secret

Or use the default-env.json with the destination from BTP:

{
  "VCAP_SERVICES": {
    "xsuaa": [
      {
        "name": "mcp-uaa",
        "label": "xsuaa",
        "tags": ["xsuaa"],
        "credentials":<btp xssuaa credentials>
      }
    ],
    "destination": [
      {
        "name": "mcp-dest",
        "label": "destination",
        "tags": ["destination"],
        "credentials":<btp destination credentials>
      }
    ],
    "connectivity": [
      {
        "name": "mcp-conn",
        "label": "connectivity",
        "tags": ["connectivity"],
        "credentials": <btp connectivity credentials>
      }
    ]
  }
}

Then run:

npm install
npm start

Deploying to SAP BTP

# Build MTA archive
npm run build:btp

# Deploy to Cloud Foundry
npm run deploy:btp

The MTA deployment creates three service instances (Destination, Connectivity, XSUAA) and binds them to your application. The health check endpoint at /health is used by Cloud Foundry to monitor application status.

Example Conversations

Here are some example prompts you can use with AI assistants:

User: "Show me all integration packages"
AI: Calls IntegrationPackages_list

User: "What iFlows are in the SAP_HCI_Monitoring package?"
AI: Calls IntegrationPackages_IntegrationDesigntimeArtifacts_list
    with path: "('SAP_HCI_Monitoring')"

User: "Show me failed messages from the last 24 hours"
AI: Calls MessageProcessingLogs_list
    with path: "?$filter=Status eq 'FAILED' and LogEnd gt datetime'2026-03-11T00:00:00'"

User: "What's the error for message ID abc123?"
AI: Calls MessageProcessingLogs_ErrorInformations_list
    with path: "('abc123')"

Project Structure

ci-mcp-server/
├── ci-api-config.json      # All 32 entity set definitions
├── package.json            # Single dependency: odata-mcp-proxy
├── mta.yaml                # BTP Cloud Foundry deployment
├── xs-security.json        # XSUAA roles (Viewer/Editor/Admin)
└── default-env.json        # Local dev credentials (gitignored)

Links

  • CI MCP Server (GitHub): https://github.com/lemaiwo/ci-mcp-server
  • OData MCP Proxy (npm): https://www.npmjs.com/package/odata-mcp-proxy
  • OData MCP Proxy (GitHub): https://github.com/lemaiwo/odata-mcp-proxy
  • SAP CPI OData API Reference: SAP Business Accelerator Hub

entry.title: BTP MCP Server: Managing Your SAP BTP Landscape with AI Assistants
entry.content:
Introduction

Managing an SAP BTP landscape involves navigating multiple cockpit screens: creating subaccounts, assigning entitlements, provisioning environments, managing role collections. What if you could do all of this through natural language conversations with your AI assistant?

The BTP MCP Server exposes four SAP BTP Core Services APIs as MCP tools: Accounts, Entitlements, Provisioning, and XSUAA Authorization. Built on the odata-mcp-proxy npm package, it requires zero custom code. Read more about the odata-mcp-proxy here: https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-members/odata-mcp-proxy-introduction/ba-p/14348684 

What Can You Do With It?

Accounts Management

  • View global account details
  • Create, update, and delete subaccounts
  • Organize subaccounts into directories

Entitlements

  • List and update service plan assignments
  • View allowed data centers per service

Environment Provisioning

  • List, create, and delete environment instances (Cloud Foundry orgs)
  • View available environment types

XSUAA Authorization

  • Browse registered applications
  • Full CRUD on role collections
  • Manage roles and view role templates

 

Entity Sets

Entity Set

API

Destination

Operations

GlobalAccount

CIS Accounts

BTP_ACCOUNTS_SERVICE

list

Subaccounts

CIS Accounts

BTP_ACCOUNTS_SERVICE

full CRUD

Directories

CIS Accounts

BTP_ACCOUNTS_SERVICE

full CRUD

Assignments

CIS Entitlements

BTP_ENTITLEMENTS_SERVICE

list, update

AllowedDataCenters

CIS Entitlements

BTP_ENTITLEMENTS_SERVICE

list

EnvironmentInstances

CIS Provisioning

BTP_PROVISIONING_SERVICE

list, get, create, delete

AvailableEnvironments

CIS Provisioning

BTP_PROVISIONING_SERVICE

list

Applications

XSUAA

BTP_XSUAA_SERVICE

list, get

RoleCollections

XSUAA

BTP_XSUAA_SERVICE

full CRUD

Roles

XSUAA

BTP_XSUAA_SERVICE

list, get, create, delete

RoleTemplates

XSUAA

BTP_XSUAA_SERVICE

list, get

Configuration

This server uses four different API groups, each with its own BTP destination. All are REST APIs (csrfProtected: false):

{
  "apis": [
    { "name": "cis-accounts",    "destination": "BTP_ACCOUNTS_SERVICE",      "pathPrefix": "/accounts/v1" },
    { "name": "cis-entitlements", "destination": "BTP_ENTITLEMENTS_SERVICE",  "pathPrefix": "/entitlements/v1" },
    { "name": "cis-provisioning", "destination": "BTP_PROVISIONING_SERVICE",  "pathPrefix": "/provisioning/v1" },
    { "name": "xsuaa-authz",      "destination": "BTP_XSUAA_SERVICE",         "pathPrefix": "/sap/rest/authorization/v2" }
  ]
}

Setting Up the BTP Destinations

This server requires four destinations, one for each BTP Core Service. Create them in your BTP Cockpit under Connectivity > Destinations:

1. BTP_ACCOUNTS_SERVICE

Property

Value

URL

https://accounts-service.cfapps.<region>.hana.ondemand.com

Authentication

OAuth2ClientCredentials

Token URL

From CIS (Central) service key

2. BTP_ENTITLEMENTS_SERVICE

Property

Value

URL

https://entitlements-service.cfapps.<region>.hana.ondemand.com

Authentication

OAuth2ClientCredentials

Token URL

From CIS (Central) service key

3. BTP_PROVISIONING_SERVICE

Property

Value

URL

https://provisioning-service.cfapps.<region>.hana.ondemand.com

Authentication

OAuth2ClientCredentials

Token URL

From CIS (Central) service key

4. BTP_XSUAA_SERVICE

Property

Value

URL

https://<subdomain>.authentication.<region>.hana.ondemand.com

Authentication

OAuth2ClientCredentials

Token URL

Same as URL + /oauth/token

Tip: The CIS (Cloud Management) service keys contain URLs for accounts, entitlements, and provisioning services. Create a service instance of "cis" with plan "central" to get all three.

Deploying to SAP BTP

npm install
npm run build:btp
npm run deploy:btp

Links

  • BTP MCP Server (GitHub): https://github.com/lemaiwo/btp-mcp-server
  • OData MCP Proxy (npm): https://www.npmjs.com/package/odata-mcp-proxy
  • OData MCP Proxy (GitHub): https://github.com/lemaiwo/odata-mcp-proxy

entry.title: BTP Cloud Foundry MCP Server: Managing CF Environments with AI
entry.content:
Introduction

Cloud Foundry on SAP BTP is where your applications live. Managing apps, services, spaces, routes, and all the surrounding infrastructure typically means switching between the CF CLI, the BTP Cockpit and various dashboards.

The BTP Cloud Foundry MCP Server exposes the Cloud Foundry V3 API through MCP, giving AI assistants full control over your CF environment. With 28 entity sets covering apps, services, networking, organizations, quotas, and platform settings, it's the most comprehensive MCP server in this series.

Built on the odata-mcp-proxy npm package,  with zero custom code. Read more about the odata-mcp-proxy here:https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-members/odata-mcp-proxy-introduction/ba-p/14348684 

What Can You Do With It?

Applications & Processes

  • List, create, update, delete, start, and stop applications
  • Create builds (stage packages into droplets)
  • Manage deployments (rolling, canary)
  • Scale processes (instances, memory, disk)
  • Run one-off tasks
  • Upload packages (source code or Docker images)

Organizations & Spaces

  • Full CRUD on organizations and spaces
  • Manage roles (space developer, org manager, etc.)
  • User management

Services

  • Manage service instances and credential bindings
  • Browse service marketplace (offerings and plans)
  • Manage service brokers and route bindings
  • View service usage events

Networking

  • Manage domains and routes
  • Configure security groups (network policies)

Platform

  • Manage buildpacks, stacks, and isolation segments
  • Toggle feature flags
  • Track async jobs
  • Manage organization and space quotas

Example

 

All 28 Entity Sets

Category

Entity Sets

Count

Apps & Processes

Apps, Builds, Deployments, Droplets, Packages, Processes, Tasks

7

Orgs & Spaces

Organizations, Spaces, Roles, Users

4

Services

ServiceInstances, ServiceCredentialBindings, ServiceOfferings, ServicePlans, ServiceBrokers, ServiceRouteBindings, ServiceUsageEvents

7

Networking

Domains, Routes, SecurityGroups

3

Platform

Buildpacks, FeatureFlags, Stacks, IsolationSegments, Jobs

5

Quotas

OrganizationQuotas, SpaceQuotas

2

Configuration Highlights

The Cloud Foundry V3 API uses lowercase/snake_case paths, while MCP tool names use PascalCase. The urlPath field bridges this gap:

{
  "entitySet": "ServiceInstances",
  "urlPath": "service_instances",
  "keys": [{ "name": "guid", "type": "string" }],
  "operations": { "list": true, "get": true, "create": true, "update": true, "delete": true }
}

The tool name becomes ServiceInstances_list while the actual API call goes to /v3/service_instances.

All CF entities use guid as their primary key (except FeatureFlags which uses name). This is consistent with the CF V3 API design.

Setting Up the BTP Destination

Property

Value

Name

CF_API

Type

HTTP

URL

https://api.cf.<region>.hana.ondemand.com

Authentication

OAuth2ClientCredentials

Client ID

From a CF service key or UAA client

Client Secret

From a CF service key or UAA client

Token URL

https://login.cf.<region>.hana.ondemand.com/oauth/token

Example Conversations

User: "List all apps in my space"
AI: Calls Apps_list with space filter

User: "Scale my-app to 3 instances with 1GB memory"
AI: Calls Processes_update with scaling parameters

User: "Show me all service instances bound to my-app"
AI: Calls ServiceCredentialBindings_list filtered by app guid

User: "Create a new route for my-app on my-domain.com"
AI: Calls Routes_create with domain and host parameters

Deploying to SAP BTP

npm install
npm run build:btp
npm run deploy:btp

Project Structure

btp-cf-mcp-server/
├── btp-cf-api-config.json    # 28 entity set definitions
├── package.json               # odata-mcp-proxy dependency
├── mta.yaml                   # BTP deployment
└── xs-security.json           # XSUAA roles

Links

  • BTP CF MCP Server (GitHub): https://github.com/lemaiwo/btp-cf-mcp-server
  • OData MCP Proxy (npm): https://www.npmjs.com/package/odata-mcp-proxy
  • OData MCP Proxy (GitHub): https://github.com/lemaiwo/odata-mcp-proxy

entry.title: AI Core MCP Server: Managing SAP AI Core with AI Assistants
entry.content:
Introduction

SAP AI Core is the backbone of AI/ML operations on SAP BTP — managing training executions, model deployments, and the Generative AI Hub. Its REST API covers lifecycle management (scenarios, executions, deployments, artifacts) and administration (repositories, secrets, resource groups).

The AI Core MCP Server brings all of this into your AI assistant. Ask questions like "List my active deployments" or "Create a new configuration for scenario xyz" and your AI assistant handles the API calls.

Built on the odata-mcp-proxy npm package,  with zero custom code. Read more about the odata-mcp-proxy here: https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-members/odata-mcp-proxy-introduction/ba-p/14348684

Note: this MCP server has not been extensively tested yet. The API configuration is based on the official SAP API documentation, but there may be edge cases or endpoints that don't behave as expected. Feedback and contributions are welcome!

What Can You Do With It?

Lifecycle Management (/v2/lm)

  • List scenarios and their versions
  • Browse executables (workflow/serving templates) under scenarios
  • List available models in the Generative AI Hub
  • Create and manage configurations (parameter sets for executions)
  • Run training executions and track their status
  • Deploy models for real-time inference
  • Manage artifacts (datasets, trained models in object store)
  • Schedule recurring executions with cron expressions
  • Track training metrics
  • Upload and manage dataset files

Administration (/v2/admin)

  • Onboard Git repositories with workflow templates
  • Manage ArgoCD application syncs
  • Store Docker registry, object store, and generic secrets
  • Create and manage resource groups for tenant isolation
  • View registered service broker services

Entity Sets

Entity Set

API Group

Operations

Scenarios

Lifecycle

list, get

ScenarioVersions

Lifecycle

list

Executables

Lifecycle

list, get

Models

Lifecycle

list

Configurations

Lifecycle

list, get, create

Executions

Lifecycle

list, get, create, update, delete

Deployments

Lifecycle

list, get, create, update, delete

Artifacts

Lifecycle

list, get, create

ExecutionSchedules

Lifecycle

list, get, create, update, delete

Metrics

Lifecycle

list, delete

Meta

Lifecycle

list

DatasetFiles

Lifecycle

get, create, delete

Repositories

Admin

full CRUD

Applications

Admin

full CRUD

DockerRegistrySecrets

Admin

list, create, update, delete

ObjectStoreSecrets

Admin

list, create, update, delete

GenericSecrets

Admin

full CRUD

ResourceGroups

Admin

full CRUD

Services

Admin

list, get

Configuration Highlights

Key differences from other MCP servers in this series:

  • REST API (not OData): csrfProtected is set to false since AI Core uses standard REST, not OData with CSRF tokens.
  • Two API groups: The config defines two separate API entries sharing the same AI_CORE destination but with different path prefixes (/v2/lm and /v2/admin).
  • Parameterized paths: Nested resources use URL templates like scenarios/{scenarioId}/executables, which the AI populates based on context.
{
  "apis": [
    {
      "name": "ai-core-lifecycle",
      "destination": "AI_CORE",
      "pathPrefix": "/v2/lm",
      "csrfProtected": false,
      "entitySets": [ ... 12 entity sets ... ]
    },
    {
      "name": "ai-core-admin",
      "destination": "AI_CORE",
      "pathPrefix": "/v2/admin",
      "csrfProtected": false,
      "entitySets": [ ... 7 entity sets ... ]
    }
  ]
}

Setting Up the BTP Destination

Property

Value

Name

AI_CORE

Type

HTTP

URL

https://api.ai.prod.<region>.aws.ml.hana.ondemand.com

Authentication

OAuth2ClientCredentials

Client ID

From your AI Core service key

Client Secret

From your AI Core service key

Token Service URL

From your AI Core service key (uaa.url + /oauth/token)

Deploying to SAP BTP

npm install
npm run build:btp    # mbt build
npm run deploy:btp   # cf deploy

Project Structure

ai-core-mcp-server/
├── ai-core-api-config.json   # 19 entity set definitions
├── package.json               # odata-mcp-proxy dependency
├── mta.yaml                   # BTP deployment
└── xs-security.json           # XSUAA roles

Links

  • AI Core MCP Server (GitHub): https://github.com/lemaiwo/ai-core-mcp-server
  • OData MCP Proxy (npm): https://www.npmjs.com/package/odata-mcp-proxy
  • OData MCP Proxy (GitHub): https://github.com/lemaiwo/odata-mcp-proxy

entry.title: BTP Audit Log MCP Server: Security Monitoring with AI Assistants
entry.content:
Introduction

Security auditing is critical for any enterprise platform. SAP BTP provides an Audit Log Retrieval API that captures security events, configuration changes, data access, and data modifications. But reviewing audit logs typically means writing API queries or using the BTP Cockpit.

The BTP Audit Log MCP Server lets you query your audit logs through natural language. Ask your AI assistant "Show me all security events from last week" or "Were there any configuration changes yesterday?" and get immediate answers.

Built on the odata-mcp-proxy npm package,  with zero custom code. Read more about the odata-mcp-proxy here: https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-members/odata-mcp-proxy-introduction/ba-p/14348684

Note: this MCP server has not been extensively tested yet. The API configuration is based on the official SAP API documentation, but there may be edge cases or endpoints that don't behave as expected. Feedback and contributions are welcome!

What Can You Do With It?

  • Retrieve security events (login attempts, authorization failures)
  • Track configuration changes across your BTP landscape
  • Monitor data access and data modification events
  • Filter by time range, event type, and other criteria
  • Handle server-side pagination for large result sets (500 records per page)

Configuration

This is the simplest MCP server in the series — just one entity set:

{
  "server": {
    "name": "btp-auditlog-mcp-server",
    "version": "1.0.0"
  },
  "apis": [{
    "name": "audit-log",
    "destination": "AUDIT_LOG_SERVICE",
    "pathPrefix": "/auditlog/v2",
    "csrfProtected": false,
    "entitySets": [{
      "entitySet": "AuditLogRecords",
      "urlPath": "auditlogrecords",
      "description": "BTP audit log records",
      "operations": { "list": true }
    }]
  }]
}

Note the urlPath: "auditlogrecords" (lowercase) overrides the PascalCase entitySet name for the actual API path, while keeping the tool name as AuditLogRecords_list.

Setting Up the BTP Destination

Property

Value

Name

AUDIT_LOG_SERVICE

Type

HTTP

URL

https://auditlog-management.cfapps.<region>.hana.ondemand.com

Authentication

OAuth2ClientCredentials

Client ID

From Audit Log Management service key

Client Secret

From Audit Log Management service key

Token URL

From Audit Log Management service key

Create a service instance of "auditlog-management" with plan "default" in your BTP subaccount, then create a service key to get the credentials.

Pagination

The Audit Log API returns a maximum of 500 records per request. For larger result sets, use the handle parameter returned in the response to fetch subsequent pages. The AI assistant can handle this automatically by following pagination tokens.

Example Queries

User: "Show me all audit log entries from the last 24 hours"
AI: Calls AuditLogRecords_list with time_from and time_to parameters

User: "Were there any failed login attempts this week?"
AI: Calls AuditLogRecords_list filtered for security events

User: "Show me configuration changes in subaccount X"
AI: Calls AuditLogRecords_list with appropriate filters

Deploying to SAP BTP

npm install
npm run build:btp
npm run deploy:btp

Project Structure

btp-auditlog-mcp-server/
├── btp-audit-log-api-config.json   # Single entity set
├── package.json                     # odata-mcp-proxy dependency
├── mta.yaml                         # BTP deployment
└── xs-security.json                 # XSUAA roles

Links

  • BTP Audit Log MCP Server (GitHub): https://github.com/lemaiwo/btp-auditlog-mcp-server
  • OData MCP Proxy (npm): https://www.npmjs.com/package/odata-mcp-proxy
  • OData MCP Proxy (GitHub): https://github.com/lemaiwo/odata-mcp-proxy

entry.title: Re: Unable to change movement type in Freight Order Booki...
entry.content:
@Chris1973 The information in the URLs you provided was not clear about where or how to trigger the movement. Your explanation helped me more than the links, and I thank you again for that. I still don’t fully understand the setup for the movement types, such as which SSCUI or app controls it.

 

Just FYI, I was able to trigger a different movement type. The correct field was the one above Inco. Location 1. By using the field IncLoc1ID, all locations become available to select. In my tests, the version needed to be updated to trigger the change within IncLoc1. If I choose FOB, only IncLoc1 is available. However, if I choose CIF (delivered at the port of destination), the system automatically enables IncLoc2 ID, allowing you to set the first port in IncLoc1 ID and the destination port in IncLoc2 ID.

Sales Order with CIF Incoterm with IncLoc1 ID and IncLoc2

 

 

 

With CIF, the Freight Ocean Booking is created, but it does not automatically bring the loading port from IncLoc1. I’m not sure which SSCUI or app should be configured to make this work correctly.

 

Sales Order with FOB Incoterm with IncLoc1

 

With FOB, the Freight Ocean Booking creates two stages that essentially represent a single movement, from the shipping point to the shipping port.

entry.title: 401 Unauthorized when sending notifications from CAP using SAP_Notifications destination
entry.content:
I am creating a CAP application to send notifications and configured the SAP_Notifications destination according to the documentation below.

When I call the API using Postman, it works correctly, but when I try to call it from my application, it always returns 401 Unauthorized.

I also tried calling it directly using the SAP Cloud SDK, following this documentation: 
https://help.sap.com/docs/build-work-zone-standard-edition/sap-build-work-zone-standard-edition/developing-cloud-foundry-applications-with-notifications

Documentation used to configure the destination:
https://help.sap.com/docs/build-work-zone-standard-edition/sap-build-work-zone-standard-edition/enabling-notifications-for-custom-apps-on-sap-btp-cloud-foundry#configure-the-destination-to-the-notifications-service

Additionally, I tried using @cap-js/notifications, but I always receive 401, both locally and after deployment.

Does anyone know what could be causing this or how to fix it?

Additional information:

  • Destination configuration screenshot

  • Postman request working screenshot

The configurations are the same as the ones generated in Work Zone for the notification service.

The BTP logs show a 401 Unauthorized error during the content deployment process in BTP.

If anyone has faced this issue before, I would appreciate any guidance.

  lr_result->%features-%delete = if_abap_behv=>fc-o-disabled.

entry.title: Production Version automatic switch when BOM components inventory depleted
entry.content:
Hello

I am looking for some guidance on how to handle this situation.

I have a scenario where the FG current BOM has single component (CIB - Cabinet in a box) and we are transitioning to Many Components (between 5 to 10 items to build a cabinet). We need the planned order for MTS or Production Order for MTO to automatically select the alternate BOM when CIB inventory is depleted. 

I tried by setting material priority or group in the BOM but it is not working as I expected. The Planned Order always defaults to the main Item even though the inventory is 0 and material is flagged for discontinuation.

AI suggested to create 2 production structures 1 for CIB BOM and 2nd for many components for the FG. Then create a custom program to switch the version based on material availability. MRP 4 needs to be updated. Any suggestion how to solve the above by automation vs manually switching the Alt BOM in the Production Version.

Thank you

entry.title: Re: Delay items part of curricula to become available to...
entry.content:
Hi @Gi, 

That is not possible. Once you add an item into a curricula & assign it to user, you cannot delay it to be made available to user.

Instead you can try an alternative approach which deals with blended courses that has both class & online content. You can refer to the below KBA that breifs you the details about what exactly are the options & how the users would be accessing the course/content etc.

2552108 - Access to content of blended item from class 

Another suggestion is to have the items assigned through program, where in you can restrict users in a way that they must complete items sections wise & can only start next section if they had completed the previous section.

SAP Help Document - Learning Programs: Bundled Courses in a Syllabus

Apart from these, there are no other alternatives we can suggest.

entry.title: Re: Delay items part of curricula to become available to...
entry.content:
HI @YashwanthP  & @Gi ,

I agree and disagree with what you say Yash, as "Yes" it isn't possible to do this with a single Curricula & Assignment Profile.  But it is possible if you use a combination of three Curricula & associated Assignment Profiles.

Let's say that you have the end goal of Learners being competent in "Curricula ABC" (which contains the Learning Items A, B, & C) by the Assignment Profile that has the rules to include Learners that have the "Job Code" = "JC00159" and in the "Org (including sub Org's)" = "Corp (753951)", but as you say you want them to complete the Items in order without any of the later Items being seen on their Learning Plan (even if you have a staggered Required by / Initial Period).

If you first assign them "Curricula A" (which contains only the single Learning Item A) by the Assignment Profile A that has the rules to include Learners that have the "Job Code" = "JC00159" and in the "Org (including sub Org's)" = "Corp (753951)", but do not have Learning History for Item A.

Once they complete Item A, then they will not met the Assignment Profile A rules and it will remove "Curricula A", but this is when "Curricula AB" (which contains the Learning Items A & B) is assigned by the Assignment Profile AB that has the rules to include Learners that have the "Job Code" = "JC00159" and in the "Org (including sub Org's)" = "Corp (753951)", but do not have Learning History for Item B but does have learning history for Item A.

Once again when they complete Item B, then they will not met the Assignment Profile AB rules and it will remove "Curricula AB", but this is when "Curricula ABC" (which contains the Learning Items A, B & C) is assigned by the Assignment Profile ABC that has the rules to include Learners that have the "Job Code" = "JC00159" and in the "Org (including sub Org's)" = "Corp (753951)", and the Learning History for Item B and Item A.

I know that from an admin point of view that this is messy, but the end user/Learner gets the seemless experience of only ever seeing the single Item in their Learning Plan. 

I used a simplier version of this a lot with Inductions where the First/Initial training is a Instructor Led but the Refresher can be Online (which the Instructor Led is a substitute for) and it works brilliantly.

Note: In this example I often add the preceeding Item as a prerequisite because I've found that they will try and short cut their training if they can and just do the Online Refresher training

entry.title: Quality inspection at outbound with Extended warehouse management (EWM)
entry.content:
Inspection at outbound is demanded by many customers for specific products. Inspection on EWM managed outbound delivery was not possible till S/4HANA 2025 FPS0 release. Capability of inspection on outbound delivery with EWM has been introduced with S/4HANA 2025 FPS01 release. My present blog is to discuss on the functionality and the prerequisite configurations to achieve inspection with EWM managed outbound delivery.

A typical process flow for inspection for EWM managed outbound delivery is depicted in below image.

An outbound delivery order (ODO) 80004946 relevant for Q-Inspection at outbound at item level has been picked , packed but not yet goods issued.

An inspection lot 100000000050 has been created in S/4HANA for the material FG1015 for the outbound delivery 80004946 from sales order 10005725.

If the goods issue is triggered on the EWM ODO, then an error message appears aborting the goods issue as the usage decision on inspection lot is pending or the inspection lot has not been accepted.

Pre-requisite configuration and master data are

  • Inspection object type 8 (Q-Inspection Outbound) needs to be activated at SCM Extended Warehouse Management -> Extended Warehouse Management -> Cross-Process Settings -> Quality Management -> Basics and Integration -> Define and Activate Warehouse-Dependent IOTs.

  • Inspection lot origin needs to be assigned to delivery type

  • Inspection type needs to be assigned to delivery category

  • Quality info record needs to be created for the material with customer and sales org.

This functionality also works for

  • Embedded and decentral EWM (with backend S/4HANA 2025 FPS01)
  • Delivery type LO Delivery w/o Ref.
  • Material with batch management

QM with EWM outbound is a simple and useful solution closing a gap for feature parity of EWM with IM.

This blog is based on my personal tests , observations , insights in S/4HANA 2025 FPS01 release. Will appreciate your feedback / comments.

entry.title: Re: SAP S/4HANA Service - Warranty Process - 6 months fre...
entry.content: In practice, most customers prefer to create the service order with reference to the warranty contract. That way, the contract drives the billing relevance and ensures the six‑month free period is enforced automatically. If you want billing documents for reporting, you can allow the service order/confirmation to generate a zero‑value billing request.

entry.title: Re: Production Version automatic switch when BOM componen...
entry.content: If you want true stock‑sensitive automation, the best practice is to implement the production version determination BAdI. That way, the system dynamically chooses the correct BOM alternative based on inventory, without requiring manual switching or scheduled jobs.

entry.title: Re: Master Data Material Best Practice
entry.content:
You can explore the below approaches

1) Material Master Governance
Introduce a central master data team or workflow approval for new material creation. Users request new materials, but descriptions are standardized by the governance team.

2) Description Standardization Rules
Define a naming convention (e.g., “Product Type – Brand – Model – Specification”). Train users and enforce rules via documentation or validation checks.

3) Use Classification / Characteristics
Instead of relying only on free‑text descriptions, use material classification (e.g., brand, model, type as separate fields). This makes searching and reporting easier and reduces reliance on description text.

4) Duplicate Check Tools
Implement a duplicate check during material creation. Use SAP standard duplicate check (if available in your release). Or build a custom search routine that compares new descriptions against existing ones (ignoring word order, using keywords).

5) Leverage Product Hierarchy / Catalog
If spare parts or equipment are catalogued, link materials to a product hierarchy or catalog code. This ensures consistency and reduces free‑text variation.

entry.title: Re: Review Booklet - Create a Navigation Target
entry.content:
Good day @leonor_brites 

Thank you for your questions and screenshots.

The most likely cause is that the navigation target is passing a fixed text value instead of the actual document number field from your booklet row.

In your last screenshot, the target app opens and Order is filled with SALESORDER as literal text. That means the navigation itself is working, but the parameter value the system passed is wrong. So, your target receives exactly what you maintained.

See the following reference for more details

Creating Generic Report Application | SAP Help Portal

So, if you maintain parameter name as SalesDocument and value as SalesOrder, then the target app gets the SalesOrder text, not the row’s sales order number. This is exactly consistent with your screenshots, where the target app opens but shows SALESORDER in the document field instead of the real sales order ID.

Question: What could be causing this issue?

Answer: Most likely, you configured the navigation target with a static parameter value instead of a dynamic field mapping from the booklet data provider.

Question: Is there a configuration step that I might have missed?

Answer: Usually, the missing step is not on the app you are targeting itself. The missing step is in the navigation target mapping which you must ensure that:

  • The parameter is pointing to the actual source field that contains the sales order number
  • Not to a fixed text such as SalesOrder.

See the following reference for more details

Creating Generic Report Application | SAP Help Portal

I recommend you check you setup and confirm:

  • That the Semantic Object and Action keeps the one that correctly opens the target app
  • Your Field-level mapping contains the real sales order number
  • Your Application Parameter is only used if the target app truly requires a technical inbound parameter, and then bind it to the actual row value, not a fixed label.

Here is a quick guid for your system:

In your Navigation target, open Sales order navigation target

Then check the parameter row, if the value is something like SalesOrder, remove that fixed value.

At the field level, bind the navigation to the field in the booklet data source that contains the reals sales order number, run the booklet again and click the sales order link.

See the following reference for more insights

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/a630d57fc5004c6383e7a81efee7a8bb/634525bea37b43d88a608e402221c932.html?locale=en-US

 

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris
